EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a moist Pacific Air mass, strong jet dynamics and cold front passage brought heavy snow to portions of western and central Wyoming. The heaviest snow fell in northwestern Wyoming. Some of the highest amounts of snow recorded were 27 inches at the Two Oceans Plateau SNOTEL site in Yellowstone Park, 21 inches at the top of the Jackson Hole Resort and 18 inches at South Pass in the Wind River Range. In the lower elevations of the west, amounts generally ranged from 4 to 9 inches. Heavy snow also fell around Casper in central Wyoming, where 7 to 10 inches of snow fell across the city. The snow was accompanied by strong winds over 50 mph in many places which resulted in blowing and drifting of the snow as well as some road closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in much of Yellowstone Park. Some of the highest amounts included 27 inches at Two Oceans Plateau and 16 inches at Lewis Lake Divide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A one-foot-deep, 40-foot-wide avalanche occurred on Disappointment Peak in Grand Teton National Park and carried a skier 1,200 feet down the Spoon Couloir. Two companions were able to ski to the injured skier who was buried except for his head and an arm. First aid was given and national park rangers made a helicopter rescue.

The passage of a potent Pacific cold front in concert with a favorable jet stream position, strong mid-level winds, and a tightening pressure gradient led to widespread high wind east of the Continental Divide. Some foothill locations experienced the strong wind over an extended period spreading across two days. The strongest winds were in the wind prone areas near Clark where a wind gust of 103 mph was recorded on Thursday, February 18. In northern Johnson County, damage was reported in and around the town of Buffalo where wind gusts of 71 to 81 mph were recorded. The damage consisted of three downed light poles, roof damage of varying degrees, and trees toppled. A semitruck was overturned by high winds along Outer Drive on the south side of Casper around 0745MST on Friday. Strong wind on the west side of Cody blew down at least one billboard later that afternoon. Wind gusts near or above hurricane force were also recorded outside of Casper and in the Absaroka Range. The strong westerly flow also brought heavy snow to the Tetons. Snowfall of 15 inches was reported at Jackson Hole Mountain Resort.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall at the Raymer Plot at Jackson Hole Mountain Resort totaled 15 inches.

The strong westerly flow also brought heavy snow to the Tetons. Snowfall of 15 inches was reported at Jackson Hole Mountain Resort.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet sensor just east of Rock Springs recorded a peak gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The passage of a potent Pacific cold front in concert with a favorable jet stream position, strong mid-level winds, and a tightening pressure gradient led to widespread high wind east of the Continental Divide. Some foothill locations experienced the strong wind over an extended period spreading across two days. The strongest winds were in the wind prone areas near Clark where a wind gust of 103 mph was recorded on Thursday, February 18. In northern Johnson County, damage was reported in and around the town of Buffalo where wind gusts of 71 to 81 mph were recorded. The damage consisted of three downed light poles, roof damage of varying degrees, and trees toppled. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure passing through the area followed by a persistent, unstable northwest flow brought heavy snow to portions of the western mountains. Much of the snow fell in bands, leading to big changes in accumulation across short distances. The heaviest amounts fell at Jackson Hole where 2 feet of new snow was reported and in the Salt and Wyoming Ranges where 23 inches fell at Commissary Ridge. In the valleys, the most snow fell in the Jackson Valley where 11.5 inches fell near Wilson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in portions of the Jackson Valley. Due to the banded nature of the snow, amounts were variable across short distances. Amounts ranged from just over 4 inches in Jackson to 11.5 inches near Wilson.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell across portions of the Salt and Wyoming Ranges. The greatest amount was 23 inches at Commissary Ridge. Other locations had generally less than 12 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure strengthening over the northern plains brought a tight pressure gradient to the area. Combined with strong mid level winds and favored northwest flow high winds occurred over northern Johnson county. The strongest gust was 67 mph at the Buffalo airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several gusts over 58 mph were recorded at the Buffalo airport, including a maximum of 67 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A two to four feet deep, 200-foot-wide avalanche claimed the lives of two skiers while a third escaped injury by clinging to a tree. The slide occurred on Ralph's Slide in Rock Springs Canyon shortly before 1430MST. The backcountry area is just outside the south boundary of Jackson Hole Mountain Resort. Neither victim was wearing a transceiver making the search more difficult. Rescuers located the skiers within an hour of being notified, but both were pronounced dead at the scene.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two skiers were killed by an avalanche on Ralph's Slide.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of isolated thunderstorms produced some severe wind gusts across portions of the Billings Forecast Area.

Another unusually strong February Pacific front blasted across the Billings Forecast Area on February 18th and 19th. Strong pressure rises were noted behind the front. These strong pressure rises combined with steep lapse rates resulted in high wind gusts across the Sheridan Foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 59 mph was recorded at the Sheridan Airport. Damaging wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph were reported in Story. Damage across the Story area consisted of various tree limbs along with a large pine tree being snapped. In addition, several trees were uprooted and fell on houses, as well as power lines resulting in some power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A plume of Pacific moisture combined with a strong upper-level disturbance and associated cold front to bring heavy snow to western Wyoming with high wind to the east of the Continental Divide. The heaviest snow fell across the western mountains with over two feet of new snow reported in the higher elevations of the Teton Range. Valley totals generally ranged from 6 to as much as 12 inches. Meanwhile, an increasing pressure gradient and favorable conditions for mountain wave-generated winds produced high winds in Natrona County and around Clark. A wind sensor west of Clark peaked at 95 mph during the mountain wave, while an anemometer on the south side of Casper gusted to 72 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report of 9 inches of snow was received from the community of Sage.

Some foothill locations experienced the strong wind over an extended period spreading across two days. The strongest winds were in the wind prone areas near Clark where a wind gust of 103 mph was recorded on Thursday, February 18. In northern Johnson County, damage was reported in and around the town of Buffalo where wind gusts of 71 to 81 mph were recorded. The damage consisted of three downed light poles, roof damage of varying degrees, and trees toppled. A semitruck was overturned by high winds along Outer Drive on the south side of Casper around 0745MST on Friday. Strong wind on the west side of Cody blew down at least one billboard later that afternoon. Wind gusts near or above hurricane force were also recorded outside of Casper and in the Absaroka Range. The strong westerly flow also brought heavy snow to the Tetons. Snowfall of 15 inches was reported at Jackson Hole Mountain Resort.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 89 mph was measured at the School House RAWS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved out of the Great Basin and passed across Colorado with another low developing in the lee of the Rockies. With ample Pacific moisture available, heavy snow fell across much of Sweetwater and Natrona counties. The heaviest snow fell around Casper where favorable north to northeast low-level upslope flow brought over a foot of snow to parts of the city. Nearby Casper Mountain received as much as two feet of snow. Heavy snow also fell in Sweetwater County where reports of 5 to 10 inches were common. The snow was accompanied by occasional wind gusts over 40 mph, which reduced visibility and created treacherous road conditions. This combination of snow and wind led to the closure of Interstate 80.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The cooperative observer in Wamsutter measured 10 inches of snow. In addition, the combination of snow and blowing snow closed Interstate 80 and other area roads for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front swept south across Wyoming during the afternoon of Saturday, February 13. Ahead of this front, strong mid-level winds worked their way to the surface in the lee of the Absaroka Range west of Clark. This produced a pre-dawn 81 mph gust near Clark. The combination of the mid-level winds and a tightening surface pressure gradient ahead of the front brought high winds to portions of central and northern Wyoming. Peak gusts generally occurred with the abrupt wind shift to the northwest and favored areas prone to northwest wind. The airports at Buffalo and Dubois both registered 70 mph gusts and several other locations recorded peak gusts around 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The weather sensor north of Meeteetse reported a peak wind gust of 62 mph with passage of a cold front.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent Pacific low pressure system produced a prolonged period of moderate to heavy snowfall over the Snowy and Sierra Madre ranges, with lesser amounts over the adjacent valleys and foothills. Strong and gusty west winds up to 55 mph produced near white out conditions with considerable blowing snow and near zero visibilities.  WYDOT closed Interstate 80 in both directions between Laramie and Rawlins for several hours.  Total snow accumulations varied from 3 to 6 inches foothills and valleys, to as much as 32 inches over the Snowy Range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cinnabar Park SNOTEL site (elevation 9574 ft) estimated 14 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 39-year-old man from North Dakota was killed in an avalanche in the Big Horn National Forest. Deputies from the Sheridan County Sheriff's Office were dispatched to the Hunt Mountain Road area near U.S. Highway 14 West just after 12 pm. The man was snowmobiling when the avalanche occurred. According to the Sheridan County Coroner's Office, the avalanche knocked the man off his snowmobile and into a tree which resulted in the mans neck being fractured. The man was deceased when search and rescue arrived.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved out of the Great Basin and passed across Colorado with another low developing in the lee of the Rockies. With ample Pacific moisture available, heavy snow fell across much of Sweetwater and Natrona counties. The heaviest snow fell around Casper where favorable north to northeast low-level upslope flow brought over a foot of snow to parts of the city. Nearby Casper Mountain received as much as two feet of snow. Heavy snow also fell in Sweetwater County where reports of 5 to 10 inches were common. The snow was accompanied by occasional wind gusts over 40 mph, which reduced visibility and created treacherous road conditions. This combination of snow and wind led to the closure of Interstate 80.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in portions of the area. Between 5 and 8 inches of snow were measured across Green River. In addition, Interstate 80 was closed for several hours due to snow and blowing snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient over the Yellowstone Plateau of northwest Wyoming generated gap flow out of the east slopes of the Absaroka Range. This flow produced high wind in a favored cross-wind area south of Clark along State Highway 120. West wind gusted in excess of 58 mph for several hours with a peak of 65 mph around 0515MST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several winds gusts over 58 mph were recorded 8 miles SSW of Clark, including a peak gust of 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The passage of a potent Pacific cold front in concert with a favorable jet stream position, strong mid-level winds, and a tightening pressure gradient led to widespread high wind east of the Continental Divide. Some foothill locations experienced the strong wind over an extended period spreading across two days. The strongest winds were in the wind prone areas near Clark where a wind gust of 103 mph was recorded on Thursday, February 18. In northern Johnson County, damage was reported in and around the town of Buffalo where wind gusts of 71 to 81 mph were recorded. The damage consisted of three downed light poles, roof damage of varying degrees, and trees toppled. A semitruck was overturned by high winds along Outer Drive on the south side of Casper around 0745MST on Friday. Strong wind on the west side of Cody blew down at least one billboard later that afternoon. Wind gusts near or above hurricane force were also recorded outside of Casper and in the Absaroka Range. The strong westerly flow also brought heavy snow to the Tetons. Snowfall of 15 inches was reported at Jackson Hole Mountain Resort.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 59 mph was recorded along the North Folk of the Popo Agie River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front and strong mid-level flow produced high wind in the favored areas around Clark and Casper. A tight surface pressure gradient produced several wind gusts past 58 mph on the south side of Casper, including a peak gust of 77 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind sensor along Outer Drive recorded many wind gusts over 58 mph, including a maximum gust of 77 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Favorable mountaintop conditions and northwest flow aloft combined to produce a mountain wave near Clark. The wind came from chaotic directions with speeds varying wildly over the course of several hours. The strongest gust was 87 mph near the foothills west of Clark. Another sensor along State Highway 120 south of Clark recorded a 65 mph gust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted as high as 87 mph at the sensor 5 miles WNW of Clark. A gust to 65 mph was reported 8 miles SSW of Clark.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure building southwest of Wyoming coupled with a developing trough over the eastern plains of Wyoming to produce strong southwest wind gusts across central sections of the state. Wind gusts in excess of 58 mph were common on the downslope side of Casper Mountain along Wyoming Boulevard. The peak gust at a sensor on the south side of Casper was 70 mph. Mid- and upper-level winds also favored strong downsloping wind and mountain wave activity along the Cody foothills in northwest Wyoming. Wind gusts were as high as 79 mph west of Clark, with other gusts between 60 and 66 mph from Cody north along State Highway 120.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind sensor 5 miles west-northwest of Clark had many wind gusts over 58 mph, including a maximum of 79 mph. Other strong gusts included 66 mph south of Clark and 60 mph at the Park County Sheriff's office.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level disturbance combined with a plume of Pacific moisture and favorable northwest flow to produce heavy snow across the Star Valley. Amounts of 7 to 10 inches were common across much of the valley. The highest amounts totaled nearly 17 inches southeast of Thayne and nearly one foot at Star Valley Ranch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts of 7 to 10 inches were common across the Star Valley with a maximum of nearly 17 inches southeast of Thayne and nearly a foot at Star Valley Ranch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A plume of Pacific moisture combined with a strong upper-level disturbance and associated cold front to bring heavy snow to western Wyoming with high wind to the east of the Continental Divide. The heaviest snow fell across the western mountains with over two feet of new snow reported in the higher elevations of the Teton Range. Valley totals generally ranged from 6 to as much as 12 inches. Meanwhile, an increasing pressure gradient and favorable conditions for mountain wave-generated winds produced high winds in Natrona County and around Clark. A wind sensor west of Clark peaked at 95 mph during the mountain wave, while an anemometer on the south side of Casper gusted to 72 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very heavy snow fell across the Tetons. Some of the higher amounts included 26 inches at Rendezvous Bowl at Jackson Hole and 23 inches at the Grand Targhee SNOTEL site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front swept south across Wyoming during the afternoon of Saturday, February 13. Ahead of this front, strong mid-level winds worked their way to the surface in the lee of the Absaroka Range west of Clark. This produced a pre-dawn 81 mph gust near Clark. The combination of the mid-level winds and a tightening surface pressure gradient ahead of the front brought high winds to portions of central and northern Wyoming. Peak gusts generally occurred with the abrupt wind shift to the northwest and favored areas prone to northwest wind. The airports at Buffalo and Dubois both registered 70 mph gusts and several other locations recorded peak gusts around 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind sensor 5 miles west-northwest of Clark reported a wind gust of 81 mph.

Mild temperatures ahead of the front, combined with showers and isolated thunderstorms along the front, helped to mix strong winds aloft down to the surface as the front passed. The combination of a slow moving upper level low passing over Utah and Colorado, powerful jet dynamics, and favorable northerly upslope flow produced very heavy snow across portions of western and central Wyoming. Fremont and Natrona counties were the hardest hit areas. Around two feet of snow fell in Lander and the surrounding foothills with 12 to 18 inches around Riverton. The 15.4 inches of snow measured by the cooperative observer in Riverton was the most in a single storm since 1999. In Natrona County, 10 to 18 inches fell in and around Casper with up to 30 inches on Casper Mountain. More impressively, most of the snow in Natrona County fell in a 12-hour period. Widespread snowfall totals of 12 to 20 inches were recorded in the Wind River, Salt River, Wyoming, and Absaroka mountain ranges. The heavy snow, falling temperatures, and gusty wind of 15 to 25 mph combined to close several roads at the height of the storm. These roads included interstates 25 and 90, US Highway 20/26, and State Highway 28 over South Pass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell throughout Natrona County, with highest amounts in the foothills of Casper Mountain. Total amounts ranged from 6 inches at Midwest, to 10.8 inches at the Casper airport, to 18 inches along Outer Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moist upslope northerly flow and a stalled front along the Interstate 80 corridor in southeast Wyoming produced heavy snowfall.  Gusty north to northwest winds of 25 to 35 mph produced areas of blowing snow and poor visibilities. Snow totals ranged from half a foot to just over three feet, highest over the Snowy and Sierra Madre mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Medicine Bow SNOTEL site (elevation 10500 ft) estimated 37.8 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a long stretch of mild and mainly dry weather, a Pacific storm with copious amounts of moisture combined with favorable jet dynamics to bring heavy snow to the western mountains. The heaviest amounts fell in the Teton Range where snow totals of 12 to 18 inches fell in about 24 hours at the Jackson Hole Mountain Resort. Over a foot of snow also fell in portions of the Salt River and Wyoming ranges to the south, and in Yellowstone National Park to the north. Valley locations also received heavy snow of up to 8 inches. Meanwhile, wind gusts over 70 mph were recorded for about 90 minutes late in the evening of March 13 on the east side of the Absaroka Range. A mesonet station west of Clark clocked a peak gust of 90 mph at 2137MST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous wind gusts over 58 mph were reported west of Clark, including a maximum gust of 90 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unstable northwest flow over western Wyoming formed a convective snow band that dropped heavy snow over portions of the Star Valley, particularly the north half of the valley. Snowfall totals of 7 to 9 inches were common around Thayne and Star Valley Ranch. Meanwhile, south of the band only light snow fell, with Afton receiving just over an inch of new snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 7 to 9 inches fell around Thayne and Star Valley Ranch.

An upper low in the Pacific Northwest tracked to eastern Colorado and brought a cold front and favorable northerly upslope flow to central Wyoming. The heaviest snow fell in the eastern Wind River Mountains where 16 inch snow totals were common. The upslope also favored Casper Mountain which received about 14 inches of snow, and the Lander Foothills where 6 to 10 inches were measured.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Casper Mountain SNOTEL, 14 inches of new snow was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a slow moving upper level low passing over Utah and Colorado, powerful jet dynamics, and favorable northerly upslope flow produced very heavy snow across portions of western and central Wyoming. Fremont and Natrona counties were the hardest hit areas. Around two feet of snow fell in Lander and the surrounding foothills with 12 to 18 inches around Riverton. The 15.4 inches of snow measured by the cooperative observer in Riverton was the most in a single storm since 1999. In Natrona County, 10 to 18 inches fell in and around Casper with up to 30 inches on Casper Mountain. More impressively, most of the snow in Natrona County fell in a 12-hour period. Widespread snowfall totals of 12 to 20 inches were recorded in the Wind River, Salt River, Wyoming, and Absaroka mountain ranges. The heavy snow, falling temperatures, and gusty wind of 15 to 25 mph combined to close several roads at the height of the storm. These roads included interstates 25 and 90, US Highway 20/26, and State Highway 28 over South Pass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 28 over South Pass was closed for over 18 hours due to heavy snow and blowing snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a slow moving upper level low passing over Utah and Colorado, powerful jet dynamics, and favorable northerly upslope flow produced very heavy snow across portions of western and central Wyoming. Fremont and Natrona counties were the hardest hit areas. Around two feet of snow fell in Lander and the surrounding foothills with 12 to 18 inches around Riverton. The 15.4 inches of snow measured by the cooperative observer in Riverton was the most in a single storm since 1999. In Natrona County, 10 to 18 inches fell in and around Casper with up to 30 inches on Casper Mountain. More impressively, most of the snow in Natrona County fell in a 12-hour period. Widespread snowfall totals of 12 to 20 inches were recorded in the Wind River, Salt River, Wyoming, and Absaroka mountain ranges. The heavy snow, falling temperatures, and gusty wind of 15 to 25 mph combined to close several roads at the height of the storm. These roads included interstates 25 and 90, US Highway 20/26, and State Highway 28 over South Pass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around Thermopolis 6 to 8 inches of snow was measured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Westerly winds as high as 70 mph produced considerable blowing snow which resulted in ground blizzard conditions for the wind prone areas of southeast Wyoming.  Visibilities were frequently reduced to near zero at the Interstate 80 summit and Arlington area, and Interstate 25 between Chugwater and Wheatland.  These adverse conditions closed both interstate highways for several hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The WYDOT sensor at Bordeaux measured peak wind gusts of 60 mph and near zero visibilities in blowing snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of deep snowpack, a clear sky, light wind and an arctic air mass brought extremely cold temperatures to portions of western and central Wyoming. Low temperatures of -25F or below occurred in Sublette, Natrona and Lincoln Counties as well as in Yellowstone Park. The low temperature at the Casper airport on the morning of December 8th was especially noteworthy since it was the 6th coldest temperature ever recorded and the coldest since December 1990.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The temperature fell to -33 at the Casper Airport. This was the earliest in the winter it had been this cold, the 6th coldest temperature ever recorded and the coldest temperature since December 1990.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and moist flow off of the Pacific combined with favorable jet dynamics to produce heavy snow across much of western Wyoming. The heaviest snow well in the western mountains where 29 inches fell at the Jackson Hole Ski Resort, 24 inches in the Salt and Wyoming Range and 20 inches in southwestern Yellowstone Park. Heavy snow also fell in the Jackson and Star Valleys where 15 inches was measured near Wilson and at the Star Valley Ranch. Meanwhile, strong mid level winds mixing to the surface brought high winds to portions of Fremont and Natrona Counties. The strongest winds were along Outer Drive south of Casper where a gust to 79 mph was recorded. This strong wind brought blowing and drifting and snow that caused travel problems.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in portions of Yellowstone Park. The highest accumuation was 20 inches at the Lewis Lake Divide SNOTEL site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of a cold front moving across the area, lee side cyclogenesis and powerful jet dynamics produced heavy snow across many portions of Wyoming. The snow hampered holiday travel in many areas. The worst conditions were across Johnson County where blizzard conditions developed and Interstate 90 was closed for several hours during Christmas Day. West of the Divide, the heaviest snow fell across the Jackson Valley where many areas had a foot of new snow. Meanwhile, East of the Divide the heaviest snow fell from Riverton and Worland to Casper where from 6 to 10 inches of new snow fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in the Jackson Valley. Snowfall amounts ranged from 4 inches at Alta to 15 inches around Wilson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of deep snowpack, a clear sky, light wind and an arctic air mass brought extremely cold temperatures to portions of western and central Wyoming. Low temperatures of -25F or below occurred in Sublette, Natrona and Lincoln Counties as well as in Yellowstone Park. The low temperature at the Casper airport on the morning of December 8th was especially noteworthy since it was the 6th coldest temperature ever recorded and the coldest since December 1990.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The low temperature fell to -25 at the Box Y ranch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moving south across Johnson County produced favorable north-northwest wind along the eastern foothills of the Bighorn Mountains. Rain showers aided in mixing strong winds aloft to the surface in the wake of the front. Wind gusts at the Johnson County Airport north of Buffalo routinely topped 58 mph. The peak gust of 66 mph occurred at 0928MST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several wind gusts past 58 mph were recorded at the Johnson County Airport near Buffalo, including a maximum gust of 66 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An Alberta Clipper dropped southeast across the Dakotas and strong winds aloft mixed to the ground. Favorable northwesterly wind aloft generated high wind along the eastern slopes of the Absaroka and Bighorn mountain ranges. The highest wind was in the wind prone areas west of Clark where gusts up to 72 mph were recorded. High wind also occurred across northern Johnson County with a 58 mph gust measured at the Johnson County Airport near Buffalo. Farther south in Johnson County, sustained wind of up to 44 mph was clocked for more than a few hours during the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts as high as 72 mph where recorded in the vicinity of Clark.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving upper-level low crossing Colorado, an unusually cool air mass, and persistent most easterly flow brought heavy snow to portions of central and southwest Wyoming. The highest amounts fell in the favored upslope locations from the Wind River Range, east along US Highway 287, and on to Casper. Snowfall of six to ten inches was common in many locations within this favored area. The highest amount measured was 19.7 inches at Sweetwater Station. In Rock Springs and Green River, four to six inches of heavy, wet snow caused many limbs to break off of trees which caused scattered power outages. A CoCoRaHS observer near higher terrain south of Rock Springs measured 14.5 inches of snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell throughout the eastern slopes of the Wind River Range. The highest snowfall amount was 20 inches at the Townsend Creek SNOTEL. Snowfall in Sinks Canyon was around 15 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed over the Wind River Mountains and moved into the Lander Foothills and Wind River Basin. Strong winds aloft were brought to the surface by thunderstorm downdrafts. Wind speeds in and near the Lander Foothills topped out at 62 to 64 mph. The wind spread northeast across the Wind River Basin with a peak wind of 59 mph clocked at the Riverton NWS office.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An automated sensor along the North Fork of the Popo Agie River had a peak wind of 64 mph at 1225MST. This gust was followed at 1228MST by a 62 mph gust at the ASOS at Lander Hunt Field.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of a cold front moving across the area, lee side cyclogenesis and powerful jet dynamics produced heavy snow across many portions of Wyoming. The snow hampered holiday travel in many areas. The worst conditions were across Johnson County where blizzard conditions developed and Interstate 90 was closed for several hours during Christmas Day. West of the Divide, the heaviest snow fell across the Jackson Valley where many areas had a foot of new snow. Meanwhile, East of the Divide the heaviest snow fell from Riverton and Worland to Casper where from 6 to 10 inches of new snow fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Across southern Natrona County, anywhere from 7 to 11 inches of new snow fell. The snow combined with a strong wind at times to bring very difficult travel conditions that caused the closure of Interstate 25 for a time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system impacted the central Rockies with periods of moderate to heavy snowfall and gusty east to northeast winds of 30 to 40 mph.  The combination of falling and blowing snow created poor visibilities and snow-packed roadways.  Interstates 25 and 80 in southeast Wyoming were closed due to winter driving conditions. Snow totals ranged from half a foot to three feet, heaviest over the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Old Battle SNOTEL site (elevation 10000 ft) estimated 12 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Westerly winds as high as 70 mph produced considerable blowing snow which resulted in ground blizzard conditions for the wind prone areas of southeast Wyoming.  Visibilities were frequently reduced to near zero at the Interstate 80 summit and Arlington area, and Interstate 25 between Chugwater and Wheatland.  These adverse conditions closed both interstate highways for several hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The WYDOT sensors at Arlington, Cooper Cove and Foote Creek measured peak wind gusts of 60 to 70 mph and near zero visibilities in blowing snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large surface pressure gradient and vigorous low pressure system moving across Montana and the Dakotas generated high winds through the wind corridors of southeast Wyoming.  Frequent gusts of 65 to 75 mph were observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The WYDOT sensor at Lone Tree measured sustained winds of 40 mph or higher, with a peak gust of 61 mph at 06/1400 MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of a cold front moving across the area, lee side cyclogenesis and powerful jet dynamics produced heavy snow across many portions of Wyoming. The snow hampered holiday travel in many areas. The worst conditions were across Johnson County where blizzard conditions developed and Interstate 90 was closed for several hours during Christmas Day. West of the Divide, the heaviest snow fell across the Jackson Valley where many areas had a foot of new snow. Meanwhile, East of the Divide the heaviest snow fell from Riverton and Worland to Casper where from 6 to 10 inches of new snow fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds increased rapidly across northern Johnson County and combined with heavy snow to produce a prolonged period of blizzard conditions. Visibility was down to near zero at times and prompted the closure of Interstate 90 for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a tight pressure gradient, a strong jet stream passing overhead and very strong mid level winds being forced to surface brought a prolonged period of high winds to portions of Wyoming. The Casper area was particularly hard hit. The wind sensor along Outer Drive had many hurricane force wind gusts including a maximum gust of 88 mph. At the Pheasant Ridge Apartments, many cars were damaged when the winds destroyed car ports. At the Sunrise Shopping Plaza, a trailer was blown over. On CY Avenue, three large trees were blown down, one falling on two trucks. Elsewhere, in the Cody Foothills, the normally wind prone areas around Clark had wind gusts as high as 96 mph with gusts over 60 mph getting into the city of Cody. In the Green and Rattlesnake Range, winds gusted as high as 83 mph at Fales Rock.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds occurred through the Cody Foothills. The highest winds were around Clark, where winds gusted as high as 96 mph. A gust to 59 mph was recorded on the west side of Cody.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tight pressure gradient and strong mid level winds mixing to the surface brought another round of high wind to portions of Park, Natrona and Fremont Counties. The strongest winds occurred near Clark where a wind gust of 99 mph was recorded. Elsewhere, Outer Drive south of Casper saw wind gusts to 78 mph and Fales Rock had a gust to 61 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The RAWS site at Fales Rock had a maximum wind gust of 61 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Periods of moderate snowfall combined with wind gusts to 70 mph created near zero visibility and considerable drifting snow through the passes of the Snowy and Sierra Madre mountains.  Snow totals were estimated between 8 and 14 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Medicine Bow SNOTEL site (elevation 10500 ft) estimated 14 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tight surface pressure gradient produced a brief period of high winds through the wind corridors of southeast Wyoming.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The WYDOT sensor at Foote Creek measured sustained winds of 40 mph or higher.

The combination of falling and blowing snow created poor visibilities and snow-packed roadways.  Interstates 25 and 80 in southeast Wyoming were closed due to winter driving conditions. Snow totals ranged from half a foot to three feet, heaviest over the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An observer two miles east-southeast of Cheyenne measured 9.5 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moist upslope northerly flow and a stalled front along the Interstate 80 corridor in southeast Wyoming produced heavy snowfall.  Gusty north to northwest winds of 25 to 35 mph produced areas of blowing snow and poor visibilities. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist southerly flow combined with an upper-level disturbance approaching from the west to trigger thunderstorms that moved northeast from Utah and became severe in southern Wyoming. Quarter-sized hail was reported from Green River to Rock Springs in Sweetwater County. Up to 400 customers were without power at the peak of the storm. Another severe thunderstorm moved into Lincoln County and dropped hail up to the size of ping pong balls in and around Kemmerer.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were numerous reports of one inch diameter hail across Green River and Rock Springs. Trees were stripped of leaves and the hail accumulated on the ground to a depth of a couple of inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist southerly flow combined with an upper-level disturbance approaching from the west to trigger thunderstorms that moved northeast from Utah and became severe in southern Wyoming. Quarter-sized hail was reported from Green River to Rock Springs in Sweetwater County. Up to 400 customers were without power at the peak of the storm. Another severe thunderstorm moved into Lincoln County and dropped hail up to the size of ping pong balls in and around Kemmerer.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm dropped hail the size of quarters and ping pong balls in Kemmerer and at the Port of Entry on the outskirts of town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level disturbance riding over the top of a ridge centered south of Wyoming helped to trigger some early evening thunderstorms across Big Horn and Natrona counties. Strong wind shear and a dry lower atmosphere favored strong outflow wind across Big Horn County. To the south, a surge of low-level moisture in Natrona County enabled the storms to produce hail and briefy heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported penny-sized hail on Casper Mountain.

An upper-level disturbance moving through west-northwest flow aloft combined with daytime heating to generate two rounds of thunderstorms across central and northern Wyoming. The first round produced fast-moving thunderstorms that brought forth strong outflow wind in western Natrona County. The second round found a more favorable atmosphere for producing hail across northern Johnson County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quick-moving showers and thunderstorms aligned in a broken line from Johnson County southwestward into Fremont County. One thunderstorm generated outflow wind that produced a 64 mph wind gust at a mesonet sensor near Hiland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Tokewanna Fire, located just on the Wyoming side of the Utah/Wyoming border,  started on July 28, and damaged several structures before it was contained. Note that this episode continued into August.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Tokewanna Fire was discovered on the afternoon of July 28, about 15 miles south of Mountain View, Wyoming. The fire burned approximately 1,300 acres and destroyed 8 structures in the Tokewanna Estates Subdivision. The fire forced the evacuation of 140 homes and the Uinta County Youth Camp, and also closed portions of Highways 271 and 410. One firefighter on the wildfire suffered burns from the fire. The fire was 100% contained as of 6 p.m. MDT Saturday, August 6.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed over the Absaroka Mountains during the mid-afternoon hours and tracked northeast across the Big Horn Basin. The lower atmosphere over the basin favored strong outflow wind as temperature-dew point spreads were more than 50F. One storm generated a wind gust to 60 mph at the South Big Horn County Airport near Greybull.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at the South Big Horn County Airport just north of Greybull measured a wind gust to 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level disturbance riding over the top of a ridge centered south of Wyoming helped to trigger some early evening thunderstorms across Big Horn and Natrona counties. Strong wind shear and a dry lower atmosphere favored strong outflow wind across Big Horn County. To the south, a surge of low-level moisture in Natrona County enabled the storms to produce hail and briefy heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A video was posted on social media showing several large tree limbs that were brought down from thunderstorm outflow about one mile east of Otto. The outflow was associated with a thunderstorm tracking north of Otto.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An extremely slow-moving area of low pressure brought significant rainfall to the Wind River Basin during a 48-hour period from Friday evening, May 6, through Sunday evening, May 8. Showers and thunderstorms repeatedly tracked to the northwest along a track parallel to the Wind River Range. Rainfall totals of 2.50��� to 4.50��� were common within a 50-mile-long, 15-mile-wide swath along and northeast of the foothills of the Wind River Range. The heavy rainfall produced widespread flooding and caused the Little Wind River to reach its third highest all-time crest at 10.19 feet. It was also the first time the river crested over action stage of 7 feet in the month of May. Flooding along the river subsided on Tuesday, May 10.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread heavy rain of 2.50 to 4.50 fell in a broad swath across Fremont County during a 48-hour period from Friday evening, May 6, through Sunday evening, May 8. There was a peak rainfall amount of 6.88��� at a rain gauge about 6 miles west of Fort Washakie on the Little Wind River. The heavy rain fell over the lower elevations of the Wind River Basin and in the lower foothills of the Wind River Range.||The heaviest rainfall occurred between 1900MST Friday, May 6, and 1700MST Saturday, May 7. The rain gauge at Lander Hunt Field reported 3.82��� in this 22-hour period, with 1.12��� falling between 0800MST and 1100MST Saturday. The heavy rainfall contributed to significant rises along the Little Wind River and its tributaries, particularly the Little Popo Agie River through Lyons Valley. The rapid river rises produced substantial flooding from Lyons Valley, to Hudson, to Arapahoe.||Overland flow was a significant contributor to the rapidly rising rivers. Recent rains had left soils relatively wet, so as rainfall piled up on Saturday morning water began to flow across fields, overwhelm ditches, and fill ravines. These waters made their way to the larger rivers and quickly filled them. By early Saturday afternoon, there were reports of water covering low-lying areas of roads north and northwest of Lander. The rain was so heavy that water seeped through the walls at the hospital in Lander prompting the evacuation of patients. Some basement flooding was reported in residential areas of Lander. By mid-afternoon Saturday, reports were received of flood waters on the Little Popo Agie River surrounding homes in Lyons Valley. Several rural roads were either covered with water or damaged by the flood waters in the valley. The flood waters on this tributary combined with the already high water on the Popo Agie River to cause water levels to surge in Hudson late Saturday evening and early Sunday morning. The subsequent flooding damaged or destroyed several mobile homes along the river on the west side of Hudson. Sunday afternoon saw the retreat of water levels on these rivers with a subsequent decrease in flood-related problems.||Multiple tributaries and overland flow to small creeks, streams, and ditches quickly filled the Little Wind River near Arapahoe downstream to around Riverton. The river topped flood stage of 8 feet at 0605MST Sunday, May 8, and climbed about 1 foot every three to four hours during the morning and early afternoon. The  river crested at 10.19 feet between 1545MST and 1600MST Sunday as residents along the river scrambled to fill sandbags and move property to a safer location. Flood waters on the Little Wind River retreated slowly on Monday, May 9, before falling below flood stage at 0120MST on Tuesday, May 10. The river fell below action stage by the end of Tuesday.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed over central Wyoming during the late afternoon of Tuesday, May 24, ahead of an upper-level low. The environment was favorable for gusty outflow wind as temperature-dew point spreads approached 40F. Wind gusts of 63 mph and 67 mph were reported in western Natrona County. Low-level north to northeast wind and more unstable air over Johnson County led to the development of hail-producing thunderstorms. One storm became severe and produced half dollar sized hail as it approached the Johnson-Campbell County line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported half dollar sized hail at a rural ranch near the Powder River north of Sussex in far eastern Johnson County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of a cold front moving across the area, lee side cyclogenesis and powerful jet dynamics produced heavy snow across many portions of Wyoming. The snow hampered holiday travel in many areas. The worst conditions were across Johnson County where blizzard conditions developed and Interstate 90 was closed for several hours during Christmas Day. West of the Divide, the heaviest snow fell across the Jackson Valley where many areas had a foot of new snow. Meanwhile, East of the Divide the heaviest snow fell from Riverton and Worland to Casper where from 6 to 10 inches of new snow fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There are numerous reports of 7 to 10 inches of new snow around the city of Worland.

The Whit Fire exploded near Wapiti, Wyoming, about 12 miles west of Cody, on Tuesday, August 2. The fire grew to nearly 3,000 acres in size during the first 24 hours and evacuations were ordered for several areas. Active fire growth continued during the first week of August with the fire reaching nearly 10,000 acres by Friday morning, August 5. Approximately 700 people were assigned to the fire and aerial tactics were employed in an effort to slow the fire spread. Evacuations were lifted on Saturday morning, August 6. The fire was largely contained by Thursday, August 11, and basically out by August 18. All said, the Whit Fire torched 12,387 acres and destroyed one home and seven outbuildings. The cause of the fire was unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Tokewanna Fire, located just on the Wyoming side of the Utah/Wyoming border,  started on July 28, and damaged several structures before it was contained. Note that this episode began in July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Tokewanna Fire was discovered on the afternoon of July 28, about 15 miles south of Mountain View, Wyoming. The fire burned approximately 1,300 acres and destroyed 8 structures in the Tokewanna Estates Subdivision. The fire forced the evacuation of 140 homes and the Uinta County Youth Camp, and also closed portions of Highways 271 and 410. One firefighter on the wildfire suffered burns from the fire. The fire was 100% contained as of 6 p.m. MDT Saturday, August 6.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms over northern Fremont and western Hot Springs counties generated a nearly 60-mile long eastward-moving outflow boundary. The boundary formed in an environment characterized by large temperature-dew point spreads of 45F to nearly 60F. The resulting damaging wind gusts exceeded 70 mph, which toppled a large cottonwood tree and snapped two power poles in Hot Springs County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two power poles were snapped three to four feet off the ground and a large cottonwood tree was toppled along State Highway 170 west of the junction with State Highway 120 northwest of Thermopolis. The thunderstorm outflow winds were measured at 63 mph at the Hot Springs County Airport and 74 mph at a mesonet station in Thermopolis.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Started by lightning on Thursday, August 4, the Fawn Fire burned steadily through the month of August. The fire ignited about 11 miles west of Mammoth Hot Springs in a remote area of northwest Yellowstone National Park. About 2,000 acres were consumed by the end of August, with a few days seeing growth of around 400 acres. Please see the September 2016 Storm Data publication for more information about the conclusion of this wildfire.

A series of upper level disturbances moved northeast across Wyoming ahead of low pressure in the Pacific Northwest. A first round of severe weather included a thunderstorm that moved out of Idaho and produced quarter sized hail at Colter Bay Village shortly after 1000MST. During the afternoon, storms erupted over southwest Wyoming with one storm generating nickel sized hail in Kemmerer, and another producing strong outflow wind in Lander.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported quarter sized hail at Colter Bay Village.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Maple Fire was started by lightning near the west entrance to Yellowstone National Park and was first spotted by a smoke jumper aircraft on Monday evening, August 8. The first substantial growth of the fire occurred Monday, August 15, when the blaze jumped from about 1,100 acres to approximately 5,000 acres in a single day. By Saturday, August 20, the fire was nearly 15,000 acres in size. Over the next two days the fire would explode to over 27,000 acres as gusty wind and very low humidity provided ideal conditions for rapid fire growth. The fire grew by approximately 5,000 acres on August 28 and 29, and reached nearly 38,000 acres in size by the end of August. The fire closed numerous trails and burned close to US 191 between West Yellowstone, Montana, and Madison Canyon inside the park boundary. Please see the September 2016 Storm Data publication for more information about the conclusion of this wildfire.

The Twin Lakes Fire was ignited by lighting about 19 miles south-southwest of Meeteetse on the evening of Monday, August 8. The fire steadily grew to about 1,000 acres in size by Saturday, August 13. Burning on public land, the fire was suppressed on the ground by over 150 firefighters and through the air by helicopters. The fire was contained by Saturday, August 20, after torching 1,579 acres.

The Buffalo Fire was started by lightning on Saturday, August 13, about three miles northeast of Tower Junction in the northeast portion of Yellowstone National Park. The fire grew by about 1,200 acres on Tuesday, August 16, and was nearly 2,800 acres in size by Thursday, August 25. Active fire conditions on August 28 and 29 allowed the fire to reach approximately 4.500 acres in size by the end of the month. Some area closures were in effect due to the fire. Please see the September 2016 Storm Data publication for more information about the conclusion of this wildfire.

The lightning-started Berry Fire began July 25 on the west side of Jackson Lake and the blaze was initially allowed to burn for ecological purposes. The fire was monitored but not actively suppressed as it spread slowly over the next three weeks reaching only 617 acres in size by Friday, August 19. However, lowering relative humidity and increasing wind whipped the fire over the coming few days. The fire had grown to nearly 1,800 acres by Monday, August 22. On that day, warm, windy, and dry conditions enabled the fire to nearly triple in size to over 6,000 acres. This led to the closure of US Highway 89/191/287 between Leeks Marina and Flagg Ranch near the south entrance of Yellowstone National Park. Several campgrounds in Grand Teton National Park were also closed because of the fire. Favorable weather for rapid fire-growth continued through Wednesday, August 24, and the fire grew to over 12,000 acres. The fire exhibited slow growth through the end of August as consumed acreage climbed to about 14,000 acres. Less active fire behavior allowed the highway to reopen Tuesday morning, August 30. Please see the September 2016 Storm Data publication for more information about the conclusion of this wildfire.

The Central Fire was started by lightning on Friday, August 26, and primarily burned in a remote area west of Lake in central Yellowstone National Park. Favorable conditions for fire growth on August 28 and 29 enabled the less than one acre fire to expand by 800 acres on Sunday, August 28, and an additional 200 acres the following day. The fire had grown to over 1,000 acres by the end of the month. Please see the September 2016 Storm Data publication for more information about the conclusion of this wildfire.

The Cliff Creek Fire continued slow growth in remote areas of the Bridger-Teton National Forest the first week of August. By Friday, August 5, containment was approximately 90 percent and the threat to inhabited areas no longer existed. In all, the Cliff Creek Fire scorched almost 35,000 acres and nearly 700 people were assigned to the fire at its peak. Please see the July 2016 Storm Data publication for more information about the start of this wildfire.

The lightning-caused Hunter Peak Fire began 40 miles northwest of Cody on Tuesday, August 9. The 650 acre fire doubled in size by August 11. Steady growth was observed over the following week as the fire grew to nearly 3,500 acres. Evacuations occurred around Squaw Creek Road and numerous roads and trails were closed in the Shoshone National Forest. The fire was largely contained by the end of August after burning a total of 3,572 acres.

An intense Pacific low pressure system tracked across the central Rockies and plains and produced a swatch of heavy snowfall and gusty winds up to 40 mph.  Much of southeast Wyoming received significant snowfall amounts from 5 to 10 inches eastern plains, to 18 to 36 inches over the mountains.  Interstates 25 and 80 were closed due to adverse winter driving conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sage Creek Basin SNOTEL site (elevation 7850 ft) estimated a foot of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Lava Mountain Fire exhibited slow growth at the start of August as aggressive fire fighting tactics were employed. As a result, evacuated residents from the Union Pass and Porcupine subdivisions were allowed to return home on Tuesday, August 2. The fire was almost entirely contained by that date, with less than 300 acres burning after August 2. Acreage consumed by the flames totaled 14,644 and the fire cost over $17 million to fight. There were no structures lost during the fire, although one home had minor damage. Please see the July 2016 Storm Data publication for more information about the start of this wildfire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong, moist westerly flow off the Pacific Ocean brought abundant moisture to the mountains and valleys of western Wyoming. The precipitation fell as snow in the mountains, with the heaviest snow of 12 to 18 inches falling in the Salt River, Wyoming, and western Wind River ranges. The position and strength of a jet streak helped to produce and sustain persistent snow in a swath from Big Piney to Pinedale in Sublette County. Snowfall rates reached up to 2 inches an hour during the late evening of Sunday, October 16, and early morning hours of Monday, October 17. Totals within this heavy snow band ranged from 8 inches to 15 inches with the greatest totals from around Daniel to Pinedale. In Big Piney, the heavy, wet snow caused the collapse of a school bus building roof. East of the Continental Divide the story was strong downslope wind. Wind gusts in the Lander Foothills topped 70 mph with a peak of 80 mph along the North Fork of the Popo Agie River. The strong winds blew over a tree in a Lander residential area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell with widespread reports of 6 to 12 inches. At Big Piney, the heavy, wet snow caused the school bus barn's roof to collapse.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong, moist westerly flow off the Pacific Ocean brought abundant moisture to the mountains and valleys of western Wyoming. The precipitation fell as snow in the mountains, with the heaviest snow of 12 to 18 inches falling in the Salt River, Wyoming, and western Wind River ranges. The position and strength of a jet streak helped to produce and sustain persistent snow in a swath from Big Piney to Pinedale in Sublette County. Snowfall rates reached up to 2 inches an hour during the late evening of Sunday, October 16, and early morning hours of Monday, October 17. Totals within this heavy snow band ranged from 8 inches to 15 inches with the greatest totals from around Daniel to Pinedale. In Big Piney, the heavy, wet snow caused the collapse of a school bus building roof. East of the Continental Divide the story was strong downslope wind. Wind gusts in the Lander Foothills topped 70 mph with a peak of 80 mph along the North Fork of the Popo Agie River. The strong winds blew over a tree in a Lander residential area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mountain wave developed over the Wind River Range and brought very strong winds to the Lander Foothills. Wind gusts past exceeding 60 mph occurred for several hours. The maximum wind gusts were 73 mph at the Lander Airport and 80 mph at a mesonet station in the North Fork Popo Agie River Canyon. A tree was uprooted and blown over in Lander.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approaching Wyoming from the west generated strong wind across western and central Wyoming. The associated pressure gradient combined with a favorable jet level wind and a very strong mid-level wind that mixed to the surface. The result was widespread high wind with frequent gusts in excess of 65 mph. The strongest winds were across of portions of Park County where wind gusts were clocked as high as 103 mph along the Chief Joseph Highway and 90 mph in the vicinity of Clark. Wind gusts over 58 mph were also reported in portions of Fremont, Natrona and Johnson counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The RAWS sensors at Fales Rock and Camp Creek reported wind gusts of 71 mph and 69 mph, respectively.

An intense Pacific low pressure system tracked across the central Rockies and plains and produced a swatch of heavy snowfall and gusty winds up to 40 mph.  Much of southeast Wyoming received significant snowfall amounts from 5 to 10 inches eastern plains, to 18 to 36 inches over the mountains. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight inches of snow was measured at Shirley Basin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong, moist westerly flow off the Pacific Ocean brought abundant moisture to the mountains and valleys of western Wyoming. The precipitation fell as snow in the mountains, with the heaviest snow of 12 to 18 inches falling in the Salt River, Wyoming, and western Wind River ranges. The position and strength of a jet streak helped to produce and sustain persistent snow in a swath from Big Piney to Pinedale in Sublette County. Snowfall rates reached up to 2 inches an hour during the late evening of Sunday, October 16, and early morning hours of Monday, October 17. Totals within this heavy snow band ranged from 8 inches to 15 inches with the greatest totals from around Daniel to Pinedale. In Big Piney, the heavy, wet snow caused the collapse of a school bus building roof. East of the Continental Divide the story was strong downslope wind. Wind gusts in the Lander Foothills topped 70 mph with a peak of 80 mph along the North Fork of the Popo Agie River. The strong winds blew over a tree in a Lander residential area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were numerous wind gusts past 58 mph in portions of the Green Mountains and Rattlesnake Range. Some of the strongest gusts were 74 mph at Camp Creek and 65 mph at Fales Rock.

Snowfall rates reached up to 2 inches an hour during the late evening of Sunday, October 16, and early morning hours of Monday, October 17. Totals within this heavy snow band ranged from 8 inches to 15 inches with the greatest totals from around Daniel to Pinedale. In Big Piney, the heavy, wet snow caused the collapse of a school bus building roof. East of the Continental Divide the story was strong downslope wind. Wind gusts in the Lander Foothills topped 70 mph with a peak of 80 mph along the North Fork of the Popo Agie River. The strong winds blew over a tree in a Lander residential area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were widespread reports over over 6 inches of snow, including 12 to 15 inches around Pinedale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm moved out of the Central Rockies into the central plains on Christmas Eve and into Christmas Day. This low resulted in tight pressure gradients across the north and northeast portions of the state. In addition, the low wrapped abundant moisture out of the Gulf of Mexico north and westward across these areas. As a result, heavy snow fell across Sheridan County. In addition, the tight pressure gradients resulted in very gusty northwest winds which created blizzard conditions across these areas. Interstate 90 was closed from Sheridan to the South Dakota line on Christmas Day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 8 to 12 inches along with wind gusts to 40 mph were reported. I-90 was closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved across Wyoming and produced some locally strong thunderstorms. Penny sized hail fell near Thayne. East of the Continental Divide, a much drier airmass produced favorable conditions for strong outflow wind. Wind gusts from a decaying shower downed power lines in Lovell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported penny sized hail southeast of Thayne.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms developed over central Wyoming in an environment favorable for the production of gusty outflow wind from high-based showers and thunderstorms. An outflow boundary ahead of this line moved toward Casper where a peak wind gust of 59 mph was measured at the airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 59 mph was measured by ASOS at the Casper/Natrona County International Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very dry airmass over the Big Horn Basin set the stage for gusty outflow wind from thunderstorms tracking east across the basin. One collapsing thunderstorm generated outflow wind that swept across Big Horn County and produced a 60 mph wind at the airport north of Greybull.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at the South Big Horn County Airport just north of Greybull reported a wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The same line of thunderstorms that produced strong, gusty winds in Utah also pushed into southwest Wyoming on December 16.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The First Divide I-80 east of Evanston sensor recorded a peak wind gust of 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across Wyoming and produced locally strong to severe thunderstorms along its leading edge. One severe thunderstorm developed over Idaho and dropped up to one inch diameter hail over portions of the Star Valley. Strong winds developed as the front swept east of the Continental Divide. Crowheart had several wind gusts over 60 mph, including a maximum of 66 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported nickel to quarter sized hail at Star Valley Ranch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Hatchery Wildfire erupted on Friday, July 22, near milepost 34 along US Highway 16 east of Ten Sleep as the result of overheated brakes on a vehicle. The fire grew quickly along the north wall of the canyon and several area closures were initiated. However, the fire was largely slowed by July 25, and was declared 100 percent contained by July 30. The fire consumed around 2,500 acres but no structures were lost.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Cliff Creek Wildfire quickly erupted on the afternoon of Sunday, July 17. Lightning-ignited the blaze on a mountain hillside northwest of Bondurant. Gusty wind quickly fanned the flames and allowed the fire to spread to over 2,000 acres by nightfall. The fire crossed US Highway 189/191 near the entrance to Hoback Canyon prompting the closure of the road and the evacuation of nearby residences and guest ranches. The fire grew by over 4,000 acres the following day in hot, dry conditions. By July 22, the fire was no longer a threat to homes and travelers and the closures and evacuation orders were lifted. However, the fire continued to spread rapidly east in the Bridger-Teton National Forest reaching 16,000 acres in size by July 26. On that date, the fire grew by almost 5,000 acres in hot, dry, and windy conditions. The fire had grown to nearly 30,000 acres by the end of July. Please see the August 2016 Storm Data publication for more information regarding the conclusion of this fire.

The lightning-caused Lava Mountain Wildfire was discovered in the mountains south of US Highway 26 west of Dubois late in the day on Monday, July 11. The fire showed little growth until around July 21, when the fire grew from about 1,000 acres to approximately 2,000 acres and caused the first of several evacuations. By the morning of July 24, the fire had grown to nearly 4,000 acres in size. Hot dry conditions over the next few days forced more evacuations in the area around Union Pass subdivision and pushed the fire to over 12,000 acres. Aerial support and a crew of over 1,000 actively fought the fire and saved numerous homes during a particularly active day on July 26. The fired slowed significantly by the end of the month, but many residents were not allowed to return home until early August. Please see the August 2016 Storm Data publication for more information about the conclusion of this wildfire.

A lone thunderstorm developed over the east slopes of the Bighorn Range and moved southeast across northern Johnson County. The storm became severe as it moved into more humid and unstable air over the plains. There were many reports during a 35-minute span of quarter sized hail associated with this storm as it moved across rural ranches north and west of Buffalo, across the town itself, and eventually near Interstate 90 east of Buffalo.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: People at a Saddlestring ranch reported one inch diameter hail.

The combination of a moist Pacific Air mass, strong jet dynamics and cold front passage brought heavy snow to portions of western and central Wyoming. The heaviest snow fell in northwestern Wyoming. Some of the highest amounts of snow recorded were 27 inches at the Two Oceans Plateau SNOTEL site in Yellowstone Park, 21 inches at the top of the Jackson Hole Resort and 18 inches at South Pass in the Wind River Range. In the lower elevations of the west, amounts generally ranged from 4 to 9 inches. Heavy snow also fell around Casper in central Wyoming, where 7 to 10 inches of snow fell across the city. The snow was accompanied by strong winds over 50 mph in many places which resulted in blowing and drifting of the snow as well as some road closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts in the Star Valley ranged from 3.6 inches in Afton to 9.5 inches near Smoot.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure passing through the area followed by a persistent, unstable northwest flow brought heavy snow to portions of the western mountains. Much of the snow fell in bands, leading to big changes in accumulation across short distances. The heaviest amounts fell at Jackson Hole where 2 feet of new snow was reported and in the Salt and Wyoming Ranges where 23 inches fell at Commissary Ridge. In the valleys, the most snow fell in the Jackson Valley where 11.5 inches fell near Wilson.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell throughout portions of the Tetons. The highest amounts where at the Jackson Hole Ski Area where 24 inches was measured at Rendevous Bowl. At Grand Targhee, 19 inches of new snow fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific low pressure system moved into the area and brought the first widespread snowstorm of the season. The heaviest snow fell in the favored east to northeast upslope flow areas. The east slopes of the Wind River Mountains had the most snow with 20 inches reported at the Hobbs Park SNOTEL. The Lander foothills had between 13 to 15 inches of snow with up to 9 inches near Thermopolis in Hot Springs County. Other snowfall totals included 8 to 11 inches in Rock Springs and Green River in Sweetwater County, 9 inches in Hyattville in Big Horn County, 10 inches near Ten Sleep in Washakie County, 9 inches in Buffalo in Johnson County and 7 inches in downtown Riverton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous SNOTEL sites reported over a foot of new snow. The highest amount reported was 20 inches at the Hobbs Park SNOTEL.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The official spotter measured 13.5 inches of snow at the Lander airport. Anywhere from 13 to 15 inches of new snow was measured around Lander.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong mid level winds mixing down to the surface produced some very strong winds in the Cody foothills. The strongest winds were around the Clark area where several wind gusts of hurricane force were recorded, including a maximum of 107 mph. Strong winds even extended toward more populated areas, where a 63 mph wind gust was reported in the west side of Cody.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many wind gusts past 58 mph were reported in the Cody foothills, including a maximum gust of 107 mph 5 miles WNW of Clark.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced penny sized hail in the Gillette area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific low pressure system moved into the area and brought the first widespread snowstorm of the season. The heaviest snow fell in the favored east to northeast upslope flow areas. The east slopes of the Wind River Mountains had the most snow with 20 inches reported at the Hobbs Park SNOTEL. The Lander foothills had between 13 to 15 inches of snow with up to 9 inches near Thermopolis in Hot Springs County. Other snowfall totals included 8 to 11 inches in Rock Springs and Green River in Sweetwater County, 9 inches in Hyattville in Big Horn County, 10 inches near Ten Sleep in Washakie County, 9 inches in Buffalo in Johnson County and 7 inches in downtown Riverton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported around 10 inches of snow near Ten Sleep. Lesser amounts were reported further west, where Worland had an average of 5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system moved into eastern portions of the Northern Plains and stalled out for nearly 48 hours. Wrap-around moisture behind the low brought snow to far northeastern Wyoming. The heaviest snow fell over the Bear Lodge Mountains and the Four Corners area north of Newcastle, where eight to 15 inches of snow was reported. Elsewhere, two to six inches were reported across the rest of northeastern Wyoming. Gusty winds produced blowing and drifting snow, reducing visibilities below a quarter mile, especially in the Sundance area.

A cold front moved across northern Wyoming during the late afternoon into the evening hours on the 16th. This resulted in a cold, moist easterly upslope flow over the Big Horn Mountains and adjacent foothills. At the same time, an upper low moved across southern Wyoming providing additional upslope flow to the mountain locations. As a result, significant snow accumulations occurred over the Big Horn Mountains and adjacent foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Area Snotels received about 13 to 15 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Cliff Creek Wildfire quickly erupted on the afternoon of Sunday, July 17. Lightning-ignited the blaze on a mountain hillside northwest of Bondurant. Gusty wind quickly fanned the flames and allowed the fire to spread to over 2,000 acres by nightfall. The fire crossed US Highway 189/191 near the entrance to Hoback Canyon prompting the closure of the road and the evacuation of nearby residences and guest ranches. The fire grew by over 4,000 acres the following day in hot, dry conditions. By July 22, the fire was no longer a threat to homes and travelers and the closures and evacuation orders were lifted. However, the fire continued to spread rapidly east in the Bridger-Teton National Forest reaching 16,000 acres in size by July 26. On that date, the fire grew by almost 5,000 acres in hot, dry, and windy conditions. The fire had grown to nearly 30,000 acres by the end of July. Please see the August 2016 Storm Data publication for more information regarding the conclusion of this fire.

The lightning-caused Arden Wildfire near Shell Reservoir and Adelaide Lake began on Tuesday, July 19, and grew to about 350 acres by the following day. The fire burned in heavy timber but was actively suppressed and only grew to over 500 acres by July 25. Area closures in the Bighorn National Forest in the vicinity of the fire were enacted. The fire was largely contained by July 28, after consuming 544 acres.

A large thunderstorm developed over the east slope of the central Bighorn Range and moved northeast across Johnson County. The storm generated strong outflow wind as it crossed Interstate 90 between Buffalo and the Campbell County line. Wind gusts reached 68 mph at a sensor along the interstate.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts of 65 and 68 mph were measured along Interstate 90 east of Buffalo as a thunderstorm moved north across the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across Wyoming and produced locally strong to severe thunderstorms along its leading edge. One severe thunderstorm developed over Idaho and dropped up to one inch diameter hail over portions of the Star Valley. Strong winds developed as the front swept east of the Continental Divide. Crowheart had several wind gusts over 60 mph, including a maximum of 66 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several wind gusts exceeding 60 mph were reported at a mesonet site at Crowheart. This included a maximum gust of 66 mph at 1339MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong and moist flow off of the Pacific combined with favorable jet dynamics to produce heavy snow across much of western Wyoming. The heaviest snow well in the western mountains where 29 inches fell at the Jackson Hole Ski Resort, 24 inches in the Salt and Wyoming Range and 20 inches in southwestern Yellowstone Park. Heavy snow also fell in the Jackson and Star Valleys where 15 inches was measured near Wilson and at the Star Valley Ranch. Meanwhile, strong mid level winds mixing to the surface brought high winds to portions of Fremont and Natrona Counties. The strongest winds were along Outer Drive south of Casper where a gust to 79 mph was recorded. This strong wind brought blowing and drifting and snow that caused travel problems.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 5.9 inches in Pinedale to 10.7 inches in Bondurant.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a moist Pacific Air mass, strong jet dynamics and cold front passage brought heavy snow to portions of western and central Wyoming. The heaviest snow fell in northwestern Wyoming. Some of the highest amounts of snow recorded were 27 inches at the Two Oceans Plateau SNOTEL site in Yellowstone Park, 21 inches at the top of the Jackson Hole Resort and 18 inches at South Pass in the Wind River Range. In the lower elevations of the west, amounts generally ranged from 4 to 9 inches. Heavy snow also fell around Casper in central Wyoming, where 7 to 10 inches of snow fell across the city. The snow was accompanied by strong winds over 50 mph in many places which resulted in blowing and drifting of the snow as well as some road closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: New snowfall in the Salt and Wyoming Range generally ranged from 8 to 15 inches; with a maximum of 15 inches at Blind Bull Summit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of thunderstorms moved east off the Wind River Mountains into an environment favorable for gusty outflow wind. The difference between the temperature and dew point at the Lander Airport was about 55F degrees just prior to the storms moving out across the Wind River Basin. Peak wind gusts at the base of the foothills in Lander topped 60 mph for over one-half hour. The maximum wind gust of 71 mph occurred at 1602MST.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several wind gusts past 60 mph were reported at Hunt Field in Lander. The highest gust was 71 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tight pressure gradient and strong mid level winds mixing to the surface brought another round of high wind to portions of Park, Natrona and Fremont Counties. The strongest winds occurred near Clark where a wind gust of 99 mph was recorded. Elsewhere, Outer Drive south of Casper saw wind gusts to 78 mph and Fales Rock had a gust to 61 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were many wind gusts past hurricane force near Clark. The strongest gusts included 99 mph 5 miles west-northwest of Clark and 78 mph 8 miles south of Clark.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a tight pressure gradient, a strong jet stream passing overhead and very strong mid level winds being forced to surface brought a prolonged period of high winds to portions of Wyoming. The Casper area was particularly hard hit. The wind sensor along Outer Drive had many hurricane force wind gusts including a maximum gust of 88 mph. At the Pheasant Ridge Apartments, many cars were damaged when the winds destroyed car ports. At the Sunrise Shopping Plaza, a trailer was blown over. On CY Avenue, three large trees were blown down, one falling on two trucks. Elsewhere, in the Cody Foothills, the normally wind prone areas around Clark had wind gusts as high as 96 mph with gusts over 60 mph getting into the city of Cody. In the Green and Rattlesnake Range, winds gusted as high as 83 mph at Fales Rock.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hurricane force winds occurred in Casper and caused significant damage. Wind gusts of 88 mph were clocked along Outer Drive with 64 mph at the Casper airport. Damage around Casper included several car ports being destroyed at the Pheasant Ridge Apartment Complex that damaged several cars, a tree falling on two trucks along CY Avenue and a trailer blowing over at the Sunrise Shopping Center.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A snowboarder went missing on December 23rd. He was walking on a cornice that failed and triggered an avalanche below him. The snowboarder was swept over a large cliff and buried in an area called Beards Wheat Field. He was found by a search dog after avalanche hazard work with explosives was performed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snowboarder was walking on a cornice that failed and triggered an avalanche below him. The snowboarder was swept over a large cliff and buried in an area called Beards Wheat Field. He was found by a search dog after avalanche hazard work with explosives was performed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of ample Pacific moisture, good upper level divergence and an approaching Arctic Front brought heavy snow to many portions of western and central Wyoming. The heaviest snow fell in the western Mountains where many locations eclipsed 2 feet of new snow, with the highest amount of 32 inches at Lewis Lake Divide in Yellowstone Park. Meanwhile, East of the Divide, the heaviest snow fell around the Cody area, where over a foot of snow was common. The snow was accompanied by gusty winds that brought blowing and drifting snow that closed many roads for a time, including Interstate 25 in the vicinity of Casper.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell in and around Lovell where 8 to 11 inches of snow occurred. Lesser amounts fell further south.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper level disturbance mixed down some strong wind level winds to the surface and brought a brief period of high wind to portions of Natrona and Fremont Counties. Wind gusts of 68 mph were recorded at Fales Rock and along Outer Drive south of Casper.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The RAWS site at Fales Rock recorded several wind gusts exceeding 58 mph. including a maximum of 68 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a tight pressure gradient and strong mid level winds mixing to the surface brought strong winds to portions of central Wyoming. Wind gusts over 80 mph were recorded near Clark and in portions of Natrona County. The strong winds blew over a tractor trailer along Outer Drive south of Casper and resulted in the closure of the road for several hours. In addition, the strong wind knocked out power to nearly 5000 customers in Casper. A gust of 88 mph along Outer Drive was the strongest wind gust ever recorded along Outer Drive. A few trees were blown down in Casper as well. In Cody and surrounding areas, the strong winds caused extensive blowing and drifting of snow that made travel difficult to in some cases impossible. A civil emergency was declared as a result.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very strong winds occurred in the wind prone areas around Clark including a maximum wind gust of 108 mph. Gusts over 58 mph also occurred near Cody and in Powell. The prolonged period of strong wind caused extensive blowing and drifting of the deep snow pack on the ground. As a result, many roads became impassable and many motorists were stranded. Some residents in outlying areas were stranded for days before roads could be cleared after the wind subsided.
